The properties in question (`endpointPrivateAccess`, `endpointPublicAccess`, and `publicAccessCidrs`) are returned by the `describe_cluster` API call. No manual inspection or SSH/RDP access is required, and all compliance logic can be programmatically enforced and audited.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: Amazon EKS Clusters - API/CLI Method: boto3 `eks.describe_cluster()` or `aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster_name>` - Properties to Check: - `resourcesVpcConfig.endpointPrivateAccess` (must be `True`) - `resourcesVpcConfig.endpointPublicAccess` (should be `False`) - `resourcesVpcConfig.publicAccessCidrs` (must NOT include `0.0.0.0/0` or any broad public IP ranges) - Pass Criteria: - `endpointPrivateAccess` is `True` - `endpointPublicAccess` is `False` - `publicAccessCidrs` does NOT contain `0.0.0.0/0` or other broad public CIDRs - Fail Criteria: - `endpointPrivateAccess` is `False` - `endpointPublicAccess` is `True` - `publicAccessCidrs` includes `0.0.0.0/0` or other broad public CIDRs

source: CIS AMAZON ELASTIC KUBERNETES SERVICE (EKS) BENCHMARK V1.6.0
title: Encrypt traffic to HTTPS load balancers with TLS certificates
description: Encrypt traffic to HTTPS load balancers using TLS certificates.
rationale: Encrypting traffic between users and your Kubernetes workload is fundamental to protecting data sent over the web.
audit: Your load balancer vendor can provide details on auditing the certificates and policies required to utilize TLS.
remediation: Your load balancer vendor can provide details on configuring HTTPS with TLS. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/userguide/data- protection.html 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/create- https-listener.html
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI) fully support automated verification of HTTPS listeners, TLS certificate attachment, and SSL policy enforcement on AWS Elastic Load Balancers (ELB/ALB/NLB). All necessary properties (listener protocol, certificate ARN, SSL policy, certificate expiration) are exposed via the AWS SDK, making manual inspection unnecessary. Automation ensures consistent, scalable, and accurate compliance checks.
automation_summary: - **AWS Resource/Service:** Elastic Load Balancing v2 (Application and Network Load Balancers) - **API/CLI Method:** - boto3 elbv2.describe_load_balancers() - boto3 elbv2.describe_listeners() - boto3 elbv2.describe_ssl_policies() - boto3 acm.describe_certificate() (for certificate expiration) - **Properties to Check:** - Listener protocol is HTTPS - Listener has a valid certificate ARN attached - SSL policy is set and enforces at least TLS 1.2 - Certificate is not expired and not expiring within a defined threshold (e.g., 30 days) - **Pass Criteria:** - All load balancers with internet-facing listeners use HTTPS protocol - Each HTTPS listener has a valid, non-expired certificate attached - SSL policy is set to a secure standard (e.g., ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-2017-01 or newer) - **Fail Criteria:** - Any internet-facing listener uses HTTP or lacks a certificate - Certificate is expired or expiring soon - SSL policy allows insecure protocols (e.g., TLS 1.0/1.1 or custom weak ciphers)

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Administration of WorkSpaces is defined using IAM
justification: The requirement—to ensure that administration of WorkSpaces is defined using IAM—can be fully validated using AWS APIs. All relevant IAM roles, policies, and their permissions can be enumerated and analyzed via boto3 or AWS CLI. While interpretation of "least privilege" may require some contextual understanding, the core compliance check (i.e., whether WorkSpaces administration is governed by IAM and not by root or unmanaged credentials) is objectively verifiable through automation. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, with the second opinion only suggesting additional specificity.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: IAM Roles & Policies associated with Amazon WorkSpaces - API/CLI Method: - boto3.iam.list_roles() - boto3.iam.list_attached_role_policies() - boto3.iam.get_policy() - boto3.iam.get_policy_version() - boto3.iam.list_users() - boto3.iam.list_groups() - boto3.iam.list_attached_user_policies() - boto3.iam.list_attached_group_policies() - Properties to Check: - Identify IAM roles, users, and groups with policies granting WorkSpaces administrative actions (e.g., workspaces:CreateWorkspaces, workspaces:DescribeWorkspaces, workspaces:TerminateWorkspaces, workspaces:ModifyWorkspaceProperties, etc.) - Ensure that only intended IAM entities have these permissions (not root or overly broad principals) - Validate that permissions are not overly permissive (e.g., avoid wildcards like workspaces:*, or overly broad resource scopes) - Pass Criteria: - All WorkSpaces administrative permissions are assigned only to designated IAM roles, users, or groups. - No root account or non-IAM entity has WorkSpaces administrative permissions. - Policies follow the principle of least privilege (no unnecessary wildcards or broad resource access). - Fail Criteria: - WorkSpaces administrative permissions are assigned to root, non-IAM entities, or overly broad IAM principals. - Policies grant excessive permissions (e.g., workspaces:* on all resources) or are not scoped to intended administrators.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure WorkSpace volumes are encrypted.
remediation: Perform the following steps to encrypt WorkSpace volumes From the Console: 1. Login to the WorkSpaces console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/ 2. Click Launch WorkSpaces and complete the first three steps. 3. For the WorkSpaces Configuration step, do the following: - Select the volumes to encrypt: Root Volume, User Volume, or both volumes. - For Encryption Key, select an AWS KMS CMK. The CMK that you select must be symmetric. 4. Click Next Step. 5. Click Launch WorkSpaces. NOTE: To encrypt existing AWS WorkSpaces data you must re-create the necessary WorkSpaces instances with the volumes encryption feature enabled as outlined above. From the Command line: 1. Run the create-workspaces command aws workspaces create-workspaces --workspaces DirectoryId=`your_directoryID`, UserName=`user_for_workspace`, BundleId=`bundle_to_build`, VolumeEncryptionKey=`AWS_KMS_customer_master_key_(CMK)`, UserVolumeEncryptionEnabled=`true`, RootVolumeEncryptionEnabled='true', WorkspaceProperties={RunningMode=`AUTO_STOP`, RunningModeAutoStopTimeoutInMinutes=`10`, RootVolumeSizeGib=`root_GB`, UserVolumeSizeGib=`user_GB`, ComputeTypeName=`STANDARD`}  2. You will receive output highlighting: - FailedRequests - Will contain information about the WorkSpaces that could not be created, and the command failed - PendingRequests - Will contain information about the WorkSpaces that were created and the command was successful. Default Value: By default, AWS Workspaces utilize Elastic Block Store (AWS EBS)by default to encrypt data at rest (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/data- protection.html) AWS Workspaces utilizes TLS 1.2 encryption and SigV4 request signing by default to encrypt data in transit. The default port for encrypting data in transit is 4172 (TCP and UDP) and is set to 128bits by default. (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/data-protection.html) For DCV, streaming and control data in-transit is encrypted using TLS 1.3 encryption for UDP traffic and TLS 1.2 encryption for TCP traffic, with AES-256 ciphers. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/encrypt- workspaces.html 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/data-protection.html 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/viewing-keys- console.html#viewing-console-details CIS Controls:
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS WorkSpaces exposes encryption properties for both root and user volumes via the AWS API (`describe_workspaces`). These properties are accessible through boto3 and the AWS CLI, making it fully automatable. The control requires checking specific fields (`RootVolumeEncryptionEnabled`, `UserVolumeEncryptionEnabled`, and `VolumeEncryptionKey`), all of which are returned by the API. Comprehensive automation is possible by enumerating all WorkSpaces across all regions.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: AWS WorkSpaces - API/CLI Method: boto3 workspaces.describe_workspaces(), AWS CLI: aws workspaces describe-workspaces - Properties to Check: - `RootVolumeEncryptionEnabled` - `UserVolumeEncryptionEnabled` - `VolumeEncryptionKey` (must be set and reference a KMS CMK) - Pass Criteria: - Both `RootVolumeEncryptionEnabled` and `UserVolumeEncryptionEnabled` are `True` for each WorkSpace (or as required by policy). - `VolumeEncryptionKey` is present and references a valid AWS KMS CMK. - Fail Criteria: - Either `RootVolumeEncryptionEnabled` or `UserVolumeEncryptionEnabled` is `False` (if both are required). - `VolumeEncryptionKey` is missing or not referencing a valid KMS CMK.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure WorkSpaces are deployed in their own virtual private cloud (VPC)
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be audited programmatically using AWS APIs. AWS WorkSpaces expose their VPC association via the `describe_workspaces()` API, and VPC resource usage can be enumerated using `describe_vpcs()` and related APIs. It is technically feasible to determine if each WorkSpace is deployed in a VPC that is not shared with other non-WorkSpaces resources by cross-referencing VPC IDs and enumerating resources within those VPCs. No manual inspection or console-only steps are required.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: WorkSpaces, VPCs, and all AWS resources within VPCs - API/CLI Method: - `boto3.client('workspaces').describe_workspaces()` - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_vpcs()` - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_network_interfaces()` - (Optionally) Resource-specific describe/list APIs to enumerate all resources in each VPC - Properties to Check: - For each WorkSpace, extract the VPC ID. - For each VPC used by a WorkSpace, enumerate all resources (ENIs, EC2, RDS, Lambda, etc.) associated with that VPC. - Pass Criteria: - Each VPC used by a WorkSpace contains only WorkSpaces-related resources (e.g., WorkSpaces ENIs, directories, etc.) and does not host unrelated AWS resources (such as EC2 instances, RDS databases, Lambda functions, etc.). - Fail Criteria: - Any VPC used by a WorkSpace also contains unrelated AWS resources, indicating the VPC is not dedicated solely to WorkSpaces.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure WorkSpaces traffic is controlled and routed through a NAT Gateway.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS exposes all necessary networking and WorkSpaces configuration data via APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). The routing of WorkSpaces traffic through a NAT Gateway can be programmatically verified by cross-referencing WorkSpaces directory subnet associations with their VPC route tables, and then confirming that outbound (0.0.0.0/0) traffic is routed through an active NAT Gateway. No manual inspection or console-only steps are required; all relevant properties are accessible via AWS APIs.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: WorkSpaces Directories, VPC Route Tables, NAT Gateways - API/CLI Method: - boto3 workspaces.describe_workspace_directories() - boto3 ec2.describe_route_tables() - boto3 ec2.describe_nat_gateways() - Properties to Check: - For each WorkSpaces directory, identify associated subnet IDs. - For each subnet, find the associated route table. - In each route table, check for a route where DestinationCidrBlock is 0.0.0.0/0 and Target is a NAT Gateway (NatGatewayId). - Confirm the NAT Gateway is in the 'available' state. - Pass Criteria: - All subnets associated with WorkSpaces directories have their default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to an active NAT Gateway. - Fail Criteria: - Any WorkSpaces subnet lacks a default route to a NAT Gateway, or the NAT Gateway is not in the 'available' state.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Web Access to Workspaces is Disabled
references: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/web-access.html CIS Controls: Controls Version Control IG 1 IG 2 IG 3 v8 13.5 Manage Access Control for Remote Assets Manage access control for assets remotely connecting to enterprise resources. Determine amount of access to enterprise resources based on: up-to-date anti- malware software installed, configuration compliance with the enterprise’s secure configuration process, and ensuring the operating system and applications are up- to-date. ● ● v7 12.12 Manage All Devices Remotely Logging into Internal Network Scan all enterprise devices remotely logging into the organization's network prior to accessing the network to ensure that each of the organization's security policies has been enforced in the same manner as local network devices. ●
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be audited automatically by querying AWS WorkSpaces configuration via AWS APIs. The first opinion incorrectly referenced a non-existent API method, but the second opinion correctly identified that the relevant information can be obtained using the `describe_workspaces_connection_status()` API/CLI method. AWS exposes WorkSpaces connection properties, including web access status, making this check fully automatable across all regions.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: Amazon WorkSpaces - API/CLI Method: - boto3: `boto3.client('workspaces').describe_workspaces_connection_status()` - AWS CLI: `aws workspaces describe-workspaces-connection-status` - Properties to Check: - For each Workspace, check the `ConnectionState` and specifically the `ConnectionStateCheckTimestamp` and any available fields indicating web access status (e.g., `ConnectionState` should not indicate web access is enabled). - If using the AWS Console, verify the "Web Access" setting under each WorkSpace's Access Properties. - Pass Criteria: - All WorkSpaces have web access explicitly disabled (no WorkSpace allows web access via browser). - Fail Criteria: - Any WorkSpace has web access enabled or not explicitly disabled.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure the default IP access control group is disassociated.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be audited automatically using AWS APIs. AWS exposes the association of default IP access control groups (such as default security groups for EC2, Workspaces, or AppStream 2.0) via its SDKs and CLI. These associations can be programmatically checked by querying the relevant resources and inspecting their network configuration properties, specifically to ensure that default security groups are not associated.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: EC2 Instances (and by extension, other services like Workspaces or AppStream 2.0 if required by the benchmark) - API/CLI Method: - For EC2: `boto3.client('ec2').describe_instances()` and `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` - For AppStream 2.0: `boto3.client('appstream').describe_fleets()` and inspect `VpcConfig` - For Workspaces: `boto3.client('workspaces').describe_workspaces()` and inspect `WorkspaceProperties` - Properties to Check: - For EC2: The `SecurityGroups` associated with each instance; verify none are the default security group for the VPC. - For AppStream 2.0: The `SecurityGroupIds` in `VpcConfig` for each fleet; ensure none are the default. - For Workspaces: The `SecurityGroupIds` in `WorkspaceProperties`; ensure none are the default. - Pass Criteria: No resources (instances, fleets, workspaces) are associated with the default security group of their respective VPC. - Fail Criteria: Any resource is found associated with the default security group of its VPC.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure CloudWatch is set up for WorkSpaces
remediation: Perform the following steps to create a Rule for CloudWatch WorkSpaces Events From the Console: 1. Login to the CloudWatch console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/ 2. In the left pane click Rules. 3. Click Create rule. 4. For Event Source, do the following: - Click `Event Pattern` and Build event pattern to match events by service (the default). 5. For Service Name, click WorkSpaces. 6. For Event Type, click WorkSpaces Access. 7. For Targets, click Add target - Click and Change the Lambda Function to `CloudWatch log group` 8. For Log Group, enter /aws/events/workspaces_access Note - You can add additional targets for other services to act when a WorkSpaces Access event is detected. 9. Click Configure details. 10. For Rule definition, enter a name and description. 11. Click `Create rule' 12. Click Create rule. Default Value: By default, The CloudWatch dashboard is automatically created when you use your AWS account to configure your WorkSpaces. The dashboard consists of the following features: View historical data using time and date range controls. Add customized dashboard view to the CloudWatch custom dashboards. Monitor the overall health and utilization status of your WorkSpaces by doing the following: View the total number of provisioned WorkSpaces, number of users connected, number of unhealthy and healthy WorkSpace instances. View unhealthy WorkSpaces and their different variables, such as protocol and compute mode. Hover over the line chart to view the number of healthy or unhealthy WorkSpace instances for a specific protocol and running mode over a period of time. Choose the ellipsis menu, then choose View in metrics to view the metrics on a time scale chart. View your connection metrics and their different variables, such as number of connection attempts, successful connections, and failed connections in your WorkSpaces environment at any given time. View InSession latencies that impact your user's experience, such as round trip time (RTT), to determine connection health and packet loss to monitor network health. View host performance and resource utilization to identify and troubleshoot potential performance issues. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/cloudwatch- dashboard.html References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/amazon- workspaces-monitoring.html 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/cloudwatch- events.html CIS Controls:
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be audited automatically using AWS APIs. AWS CloudWatch Events (EventBridge) exposes all necessary rule configuration details—including event patterns and targets—via the boto3 `events` client and AWS CLI. It is fully feasible to enumerate rules, inspect their event patterns to confirm they match WorkSpaces events, and verify that appropriate targets (such as CloudWatch Log Groups) are configured, all without manual console inspection.
automation_summary: - **AWS Resource/Service:** CloudWatch Events (EventBridge) Rules for Amazon WorkSpaces - **API/CLI Method:** - boto3: `boto3.client('events').list_rules()`, `describe_rule()`, `list_targets_by_rule()` - AWS CLI: `aws events list-rules`, `aws events describe-rule`, `aws events list-targets-by-rule` - **Properties to Check:** - The rule's `EventPattern` includes `"source": ["aws.workspaces"]` and relevant event types (e.g., `"eventName": ["Access"]` or as required by the benchmark). - The rule is enabled (`State` is `ENABLED`). - The rule has at least one target configured (e.g., a CloudWatch Log Group or SNS topic). - **Pass Criteria:** - At least one enabled CloudWatch Event rule exists in each region with an event pattern that matches WorkSpaces events (source: `aws.workspaces` and appropriate event types). - The rule has a valid target (e.g., CloudWatch Log Group, SNS, Lambda) for monitoring or alerting. - **Fail Criteria:** - No such rule exists, the rule is disabled, the event pattern does not match WorkSpaces events, or there are no valid targets configured.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure that patches and updates are performed on the operating system for Workstations
justification: While the first opinion suggests manual verification, AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Patch Manager provides robust automation for patching and compliance reporting on EC2-based workstations. SSM can both apply patches and generate compliance reports accessible via AWS APIs, making this control fully automatable for AWS-managed instances. Manual steps are only necessary for unmanaged or non-SSM-integrated resources, which are outside the scope of AWS-native compliance automation.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: EC2 Instances managed by AWS Systems Manager (SSM) - API/CLI Method: - boto3 ssm.describe_instance_patch_states() - boto3 ssm.describe_instance_patch_compliance() - AWS CLI: aws ssm describe-instance-patch-states - Properties to Check: - Patch compliance status fields: `PatchGroup`, `InstanceId`, `MissingCount`, `FailedCount`, `InstalledPendingRebootCount`, `OperationEndTime`, `ComplianceState` - Pass Criteria: - `ComplianceState` is `COMPLIANT` - `MissingCount` and `FailedCount` are `0` - No pending reboots (`InstalledPendingRebootCount` is `0`) - Fail Criteria: - `ComplianceState` is `NON_COMPLIANT` - Any `MissingCount` or `FailedCount` greater than `0` - Pending reboots not addressed

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Workspaces images are not older than 90 days.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that AWS exposes WorkSpaces image metadata, including creation dates, via the `describe_workspace_images` API. This allows for a fully automated compliance check by programmatically comparing each image's creation date to the current date to ensure none are older than 90 days. No manual inspection or console-only properties are required, and the process is entirely scriptable using AWS SDKs or CLI.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: Amazon WorkSpaces Images - API/CLI Method: boto3 client method `describe_workspace_images()` or AWS CLI `aws workspaces describe-workspace-images` - Properties to Check: Each image's `Created` (creation date) field in the response - Pass Criteria: All WorkSpaces images have a `Created` date within the last 90 days from the current date - Fail Criteria: Any WorkSpaces image has a `Created` date older than 90 days from the current date

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure WorkSpaces that are not being utilized are removed.
remediation: Perform the following to remove unused WorkSpaces based on the output collected from the audit procedure From the Console: 1. Log in to WorkSpaces dashboard at https://console.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/. 2. In the left panel click WorkSpaces 3. Select the WorkSpace ID that you have identified as not being used. 4. Click Actions, Remove WorkSpaces 5. Confirm using the Audit that this is the WorkSpaces ID you should remove. 6. Click Remove WorkSpaces From the Command line: Note that running this command does not prompt you to confirm that you are removing the correct WorkSpaces ID. 1. Run terminate-workspaces command using the ID of the WorkSpaces instance from the Audit that you want to delete: aws workspaces terminate-workspaces --region us-east-1 --terminate-workspace-requests ws-0cgsl1k23 Default Value: By default, Workspaces are not deleted after a defined period of inactivity. References: 1. https://aws.amazon.com/workspaces/faqs/ 2. https://awscli.amazonaws.com/v2/documentation/api/latest/reference/workspace s/index.html#cli-aws-workspaces CIS Controls:
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS WorkSpaces utilization can be programmatically determined using AWS APIs. Specifically, boto3 provides methods such as `describe_workspaces()` to list WorkSpaces and `describe_workspaces_connection_status()` to check for recent user connections. No manual inspection is required, as all necessary data is exposed via the AWS SDK, making full automation feasible and reliable for this control.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: WorkSpaces (AWS End User Compute) - API/CLI Method: - boto3 `workspaces.describe_workspaces()` - boto3 `workspaces.describe_workspaces_connection_status()` - Properties to Check: - WorkSpace `State` (should be "AVAILABLE") - `ConnectionStatus` → `LastKnownUserConnectionTimestamp` - Pass Criteria: - All WorkSpaces in "AVAILABLE" state have a `LastKnownUserConnectionTimestamp` within the defined recent period (e.g., last 30 days). - Fail Criteria: - Any WorkSpace in "AVAILABLE" state has no `LastKnownUserConnectionTimestamp` within the defined recent period (e.g., last 30 days), indicating it is unused.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure primary interface ports for Workspaces are not open to all inbound traffic.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the security group rules associated with AWS Workspaces can be audited programmatically using AWS APIs. The necessary information—such as Workspace associations, VPCs, and security groups—is accessible via boto3 and AWS CLI. While the relationship between Workspaces and security groups is indirect (via directories and VPCs), all required data is available through API calls, making full automation feasible and reliable.
automation_summary: - **AWS Resource/Service:** AWS Workspaces, EC2 Security Groups - **API/CLI Method:** - `boto3.client('workspaces').describe_workspaces()` - `boto3.client('workspaces').describe_workspace_directories()` - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` - **Properties to Check:** - For each Workspace, determine the associated directory and VPC. - Identify security groups attached to the Workspace's VPC/subnet. - Inspect each security group's inbound rules for entries allowing traffic from `0.0.0.0/0` (all IPv4) or `::/0` (all IPv6) on any port. - **Pass Criteria:** - No inbound security group rule associated with any Workspace allows unrestricted access (`0.0.0.0/0` or `::/0`) to primary interface ports. - **Fail Criteria:** - Any inbound rule on a security group associated with a Workspace allows traffic from `0.0.0.0/0` or `::/0` to primary interface ports.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure FIPS Endpoint encryption is enabled for WorkSpaces.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be audited using AWS APIs, specifically via boto3 or AWS CLI. The key setting—whether a WorkSpaces directory is using a FIPS endpoint—is exposed through the `describe_workspace_directories` API, which returns the `EnableWorkDocs` and, more importantly, the `WorkspaceCreationProperties` object. Within this, the `CustomSecurityGroupId` and `EnableInternetAccess` are present, but for FIPS endpoint, the relevant property is `WorkspaceAccessProperties` with the field `DeviceTypeFIPS`. This allows for programmatic verification of FIPS endpoint usage. Therefore, automation is both feasible and reliable.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: Amazon WorkSpaces Directories - API/CLI Method: - boto3: `workspaces.describe_workspace_directories()` - AWS CLI: `aws workspaces describe-workspace-directories` - Properties to Check: - In the response, under each Directory, check `WorkspaceAccessProperties.DeviceTypeFIPS`. - The value should be `ENABLED` for FIPS endpoint encryption to be considered enabled. - Pass Criteria: - All WorkSpaces Directories have `WorkspaceAccessProperties.DeviceTypeFIPS` set to `ENABLED`. - Fail Criteria: - Any WorkSpaces Directory has `WorkspaceAccessProperties.DeviceTypeFIPS` not set to `ENABLED` (i.e., `DISABLED` or missing).

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure WorkSpaces API requests flow through a VPC Endpoint
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that verifying whether WorkSpaces API requests flow through a VPC Endpoint can be accomplished using AWS APIs, specifically by querying VPC endpoints via boto3 or AWS CLI. The required properties—such as the existence, state, service association, and policy of VPC endpoints—are all exposed through the AWS EC2 API. No manual inspection or console-only steps are necessary, making this control fully automatable.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: VPC Endpoints (for AWS WorkSpaces) - API/CLI Method: boto3 EC2 client’s describe_vpc_endpoints() - Properties to Check: - ServiceName: Should match `com.amazonaws.<region>.workspaces` - State: Must be `available` - VPC association: Must be associated with the correct VPC(s) - PolicyDocument: Should allow WorkSpaces API actions - Pass Criteria: - At least one VPC endpoint exists for `com.amazonaws.<region>.workspaces` - The endpoint is in `available` state - The endpoint is associated with the intended VPC(s) - The endpoint policy allows required WorkSpaces API actions - Fail Criteria: - No VPC endpoint exists for WorkSpaces in the region - Endpoint is not in `available` state - Endpoint is not associated with the correct VPC(s) - Endpoint policy does not allow necessary WorkSpaces API actions

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure User Access Logging is enabled
remediation: From the Console: 1. Log in to the Amazon Kinesis console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/kinesis/home 2. In the left pane, click Data Streams then Create data stream. 3. Enter a name for your data stream. The name must be prefixed with amazon- workspaces-web 4. Select the desired data stream capacity and click Create data stream 5. Log into the Amazon WorkSpaces console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/v2/home 6. In the left pane click Web Portals 7. Click the link for the web portal you wish to edit. 8. Click Edit 9. Scroll to User access logging and select the Kinesis data stream you created above. 10. Click Save From the Command Line: 1. Run the create-user-access-logging-settings command aws workspaces-web create-user-access-logging-settings --kinesis-stream-arn <kinesis_data_stream_arn>. --output table  2. The output will return a list of settings ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------- | CreateUserAccessLoggingSettings | +------------------------------+--------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------+ |  userAccessLoggingSettingsArn|  arn:aws:workspaces- web:[region]:[account]:userAccessLoggingSettings/[guid] | +------------------------------+--------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------+ Default Value: By default, user access logging is not enabled. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces-web/latest/adminguide/data- protection-logging.html 4 WorkDocs This section contains recommendations for configuring the WorkDocs service and any additional resources required.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the status of User Access Logging for Amazon WorkSpaces Web can be programmatically verified using AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). The relevant settings and their association with a Kinesis Data Stream are exposed via the AWS WorkSpaces Web API, making automation feasible and reliable. There is no requirement for manual inspection, as all necessary configuration data is accessible through API calls.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: Amazon WorkSpaces Web (User Access Logging Settings), Amazon Kinesis Data Streams - API/CLI Method: - `boto3.client('workspaces-web').list_user_access_logging_settings()` - `boto3.client('workspaces-web').describe_user_access_logging_settings()` - Properties to Check: - Presence of `userAccessLoggingSettingsArn` for each WorkSpaces Web portal - Association of the logging settings with a valid Kinesis Data Stream ARN - Status of the Kinesis Data Stream (should be ACTIVE) - (Optional but recommended) Permissions on the Kinesis Data Stream to ensure WorkSpaces Web can write logs - Pass Criteria: - Each WorkSpaces Web portal has a `userAccessLoggingSettingsArn` configured - The associated Kinesis Data Stream exists and is in ACTIVE status - (Optional) The Kinesis Data Stream has the necessary permissions for logging - Fail Criteria: - No `userAccessLoggingSettingsArn` is configured for a portal - The referenced Kinesis Data Stream does not exist or is not ACTIVE - (Optional) Insufficient permissions for logging

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Administrators of WorkDocs is defined using IAM

justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that all necessary IAM group and policy information is accessible via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: IAM Groups, IAM Policies, IAM Users - API/CLI Method: - `list_groups()` (boto3: iam.list_groups) - `list_attached_group_policies(GroupName=...)` (boto3: iam.list_attached_group_policies) - `get_group(GroupName=...)` (boto3: iam.get_group) - Properties to Check: - At least one IAM group with the `AmazonWorkDocsFullAccess` policy attached - At least one user assigned to that group - Pass Criteria: - There exists at least one IAM group with the `AmazonWorkDocsFullAccess` policy attached AND at least one user is a member of that group. - Fail Criteria: - No IAM group has the `AmazonWorkDocsFullAccess` policy attached, OR such a group exists but has no users assigned.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Utilize site wide activity feed for monitoring.
justification: While the control language is somewhat generic, AWS provides robust APIs (via boto3, AWS CLI, and AWS Config) to programmatically verify the presence and configuration of site-wide activity feeds such as AWS CloudTrail and CloudWatch. These services are designed to capture and monitor activity across AWS accounts, fulfilling the intent of a "site wide activity feed." The verification of their existence and correct configuration (e.g., CloudTrail enabled, logging to a secure S3 bucket, multi-region trails, etc.) can be fully automated using AWS APIs.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: AWS CloudTrail (account-level), optionally AWS CloudWatch for log delivery - API/CLI Method: - boto3.client('cloudtrail').describe_trails() - boto3.client('cloudtrail').get_trail_status() - boto3.client('cloudtrail').get_event_selectors() - Properties to Check: - At least one trail is present and enabled - Trail is logging management events for all regions (isMultiRegionTrail=True) - Logging is currently enabled (IsLogging=True) - Logs are delivered to a secure S3 bucket - Pass Criteria: - CloudTrail is enabled and logging for all regions - Management events are being captured - Logs are delivered to a secure S3 bucket - Fail Criteria: - No trails enabled, or not multi-region - Logging is disabled - Management events not captured - No S3 log delivery configured

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure only specific users are allowed to invite external users
justification: The control requires verifying that only specific users are allowed to invite external users, which is governed by IAM permissions (e.g., actions like organizations:InviteAccountToOrganization). These permissions are fully exposed via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI), allowing programmatic inspection of which users, groups, or roles have these permissions. While organizational policy decisions about "who should have access" are subjective, the technical enforcement and verification of who actually has the permission is entirely automatable.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: IAM Policies, IAM Users, IAM Roles, IAM Groups - API/CLI Method: - boto3 iam.list_policies() - boto3 iam.list_policy_versions() - boto3 iam.get_policy_version() - boto3 iam.list_entities_for_policy() - boto3 iam.get_user_policy(), get_group_policy(), get_role_policy() - Properties to Check: - Policy statements granting organizations:InviteAccountToOrganization or similar invite permissions. - Which users, groups, or roles are attached to these policies. - Pass Criteria: - Only the explicitly approved users/roles/groups (as defined by organizational policy) have the invite permission. - Fail Criteria: - Any user, group, or role outside the approved list has the invite permission.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure publicly shareable links is not allowed in WorkDocs
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS WorkDocs exposes organization configuration settings, including public link sharing permissions, via the AWS API (boto3 and AWS CLI). source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure any user that has not accessed WorkDocs in 30 days is set to inactive.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be automated using AWS APIs. Amazon WorkDocs exposes user information and activity data via the AWS SDK (boto3), specifically through the `describe_users()` API. While the exact "last activity" timestamp may not be a direct property, it can be inferred from user metadata or, if needed, by parsing CloudTrail logs for WorkDocs activity. This process does not require manual inspection, as all necessary data is accessible programmatically.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: Amazon WorkDocs Users - API/CLI Method: - Primary: `boto3.client('workdocs').describe_users()` - Supplementary (if needed): Parse AWS CloudTrail logs for WorkDocs activity events - Properties to Check: - `LastActivityTimestamp` (if available in user metadata) - If not directly available, extract last activity from CloudTrail events related to user actions in WorkDocs - Pass Criteria: - All users have a `LastActivityTimestamp` within the past 30 days, or are already set to inactive if not. - Fail Criteria: - Any user has not accessed WorkDocs in the past 30 days and is still marked as active.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure AppStream is utilizing its own virtual private cloud (VPC)
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that AWS AppStream fleet VPC assignments are fully exposed via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). The required information (VPC configuration for each AppStream fleet) can be programmatically retrieved and evaluated for compliance. There is no need for manual inspection, as all necessary properties are accessible and verifiable through standard AWS SDK calls.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: AppStream Fleets - API/CLI Method: boto3 client('appstream').describe_fleets() or aws appstream describe-fleets - Properties to Check: For each fleet, examine the VpcConfig.VpcId property. Ensure each fleet is assigned to a dedicated (non-shared) VPC, and that no fleet is running without a VPC or in a default/shared VPC. - Pass Criteria: Every AppStream fleet has a VpcConfig with a unique VpcId that is not used by other non-AppStream resources and is not the default/shared VPC. - Fail Criteria: Any AppStream fleet is missing a VpcConfig, is assigned to a default/shared VPC, or shares a VPC with unrelated resources.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure a VPC Endpoint is set for AppStream
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the presence and configuration of a VPC Endpoint for AppStream can be fully verified using AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). The required properties—such as the existence, state, and association of the VPC Endpoint with the AppStream service—are all exposed via the EC2 API (`describe_vpc_endpoints`). No manual inspection is needed, as all relevant data is programmatically accessible and auditable.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: VPC Endpoints for AppStream (EC2 VPC Endpoints) - API/CLI Method: - boto3: `ec2.describe_vpc_endpoints()` - AWS CLI: `aws ec2 describe-vpc-endpoints --filters "Name=service-name,Values=com.amazonaws.<region>.appstream2"` - Properties to Check: - `ServiceName` equals `com.amazonaws.<region>.appstream2` - `State` is `available` - `VpcId` matches the intended VPC for AppStream - (Optional but recommended) `SubnetIds` and `RouteTableIds` are correctly associated as per AppStream deployment requirements - Pass Criteria: - At least one VPC Endpoint exists for `com.amazonaws.<region>.appstream2` in the intended VPC, is in the `available` state, and is associated with the correct subnets/route tables. - Fail Criteria: - No VPC Endpoint exists for AppStream, or it is not in the `available` state, or it is not associated with the correct VPC/subnets.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure maximum session duration is no longer than 10 hours
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be fully automated using AWS APIs, specifically by retrieving all IAM roles and checking their MaxSessionDuration attribute. The required property is directly accessible via boto3 (`get_role`) and AWS CLI (`aws iam get-role`). No manual inspection is necessary, and the check is straightforward and reliable using programmatic methods.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: IAM Roles - API/CLI Method: - boto3: `iam.list_roles()`, `iam.get_role(RoleName=...)` - AWS CLI: `aws iam list-roles`, `aws iam get-role --role-name <ROLE_NAME>` - Properties to Check: `MaxSessionDuration` attribute of each IAM role - Pass Criteria: Every IAM role has `MaxSessionDuration` set to 36,000 seconds (10 hours) or less - Fail Criteria: Any IAM role has `MaxSessionDuration` set to more than 36,000 seconds

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure session disconnect timeout is set to 5 minutes or less

These APIs allow programmatic retrieval and verification of session timeout configurations, making automation feasible and reliable. automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: AppStream 2.0 Stacks - API/CLI Method: boto3 appstream.describe_stacks() or aws appstream describe-stacks - Properties to Check: Each stack's 'DisconnectTimeoutInSeconds' property - Pass Criteria: 'DisconnectTimeoutInSeconds' is set to 300 seconds (5 minutes) or less for all stacks - Fail Criteria: Any stack has 'DisconnectTimeoutInSeconds' set to more than 300 seconds, or the property is unset

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure internet access is granted and managed through your VPC
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that all relevant VPC internet access configurations—such as route tables, internet gateways, and their associations—are fully accessible via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). There is no requirement for manual inspection or console-only data; all necessary properties are programmatically retrievable and auditable. This makes the control ideally suited for automation.
automation_summary: - **AWS Resource/Service:** VPCs, Route Tables, Internet Gateways, Network ACLs, Security Groups - **API/CLI Method:** - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_vpcs()` - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_route_tables()` - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_internet_gateways()` - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_network_acls()` - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` - **Properties to Check:** - Route tables with routes targeting an Internet Gateway (IGW) - Associations between route tables and subnets - Existence and attachment of IGWs to VPCs - Network ACLs and Security Groups for rules allowing unrestricted (0.0.0.0/0) access - **Pass Criteria:** - All internet access is routed through explicitly managed IGWs associated with the VPC. - No subnets have unintended direct internet access (e.g., via misconfigured route tables). - Network ACLs and Security Groups do not allow unrestricted inbound/outbound internet access unless explicitly required and documented. - **Fail Criteria:** - Any subnet has a route to an IGW not managed through the VPC. - IGWs are attached to VPCs without proper route table configuration. - Network ACLs or Security Groups allow unrestricted (0.0.0.0/0) access without justification.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: AWS Storage Backups
description: AWS Storage Backups is a managed AWS Service that establishes high resiliency to your cloud resources. AWS Storage Backups are like making extra copies of your important stuff on Amazon's computers. It is an excellent strategy to ensure that the data and resources you use remain available in the event of unrecoverable damage or loss to your resources.
rationale: AWS Backups enable you to back up and restore all data lost during the attack,While AWS Storage Backups provide a level of security, there are numerous methods to fortify your backups, ensuring the protection of your data and services.
justification: Both AI opinions agree that this control can be audited using AWS APIs, specifically via the AWS Backup service APIs (boto3/CLI). All required properties—existence of backup plans, their schedules, and retention settings—are exposed through the AWS SDK and can be programmatically verified. While the second opinion suggests more detail and specificity in the program name and checks, there is no technical barrier to full automation for this control.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: AWS Backup (Backup Plans and Backup Vaults) - API/CLI Method: - `boto3.client('backup').list_backup_plans()` - `boto3.client('backup').get_backup_plan()` - `boto3.client('backup').list_backup_vaults()` - Properties to Check: - Each backup plan must have at least one backup rule with a defined schedule (`ScheduleExpression`). - Each backup rule must have a retention period (`Lifecycle` with `DeleteAfterDays` set). - Backup plan status must be `ACTIVE`. - Pass Criteria: - At least one active backup plan exists. - All backup plans have scheduled backup rules and defined retention periods. - Fail Criteria: - No active backup plans exist, or any backup plan lacks scheduled rules or retention settings.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure to create backup template and name
description: To create a backup plan, select a template and specify a name for the plan. Additionally, define backup rules according to your requirements and then click on create backup option.
audit: Backup Resources: Once you've made your backup plan, it's time to put it into action and start backing up your stuff. Let's start by backing up an S3 storage bucket. To back up Elastic Beanstalk instance stored on AWS S3, we'll need to tag its Amazon Resource Name (ARN) with a backup plan. In S3, go to "properties" to attach the backup plan to the resource: 1. Copy the ARN from the console: From the AWS Management Console, copy the ARN (Amazon Resource Name) associated with the Elastic Beanstalk instance. This unique identifier will be used to tag the resource for backup. 2. Assign the resource: o After copying the ARN, return to the AWS Management Console and access Amazon Backup. o Choose the backup plan recently created, then proceed to assign the resource you wish to backup, such as the S3 bucket containing the Elastic Beanstalk resource. o Finally, navigate to "Resource Assignments" to complete the process. Choose "Assign Resources" and provide a name for the assignment. For now, maintain the role as Default. In subsequent sections, we'll explore implementing custom IAM roles and policies for your backup operations. Select the resource(s) that you want to backup. You have the option to backup all your resources, but we’re just going to back up the specific Elastic Beanstalk resource for now. The resources are now being backed up according to the schedule established by your organization.
remediation: The AWS backup vault serves as the storage location for your backups. It's crucial to manage access to these backups to prevent unauthorized access and ensure data security. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/how-it-works.html
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS Backup plan creation, naming, and resource assignment (including tagging and plan verification) are fully accessible and auditable via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). All required properties—backup plan existence, naming, backup rules, resource assignments, and tagging—are exposed through AWS Backup API methods such as list_backup_plans, describe_backup_plan, get_backup_selection, and list_tags_for_resource. No manual inspection is required, as all compliance criteria can be programmatically validated.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: AWS Backup (Backup Plans, Resource Assignments, Tags) - API/CLI Method: - `boto3.client('backup').list_backup_plans()` - `boto3.client('backup').describe_backup_plan(BackupPlanId=...)` - `boto3.client('backup').list_backup_selections(BackupPlanId=...)` - `boto3.client('backup').get_backup_selection(BackupPlanId=..., SelectionId=...)` - `boto3.client('backup').list_tags_for_resource(ResourceArn=...)` - Properties to Check: - Existence of backup plan(s) with required template and specified naming convention. - Backup rules defined as per requirements. - Resource assignments include the intended S3 bucket (or other specified resources). - Resource(s) are tagged appropriately and associated with the backup plan. - Pass Criteria: - At least one backup plan exists with the required name and template. - Backup rules match organizational or CIS requirements. - Target resources are assigned to the backup plan. - Tags on resources and backup plan match compliance requirements. - Fail Criteria: - No backup plan exists, or naming/template is incorrect. - Backup rules are missing or misconfigured. - Target resources are not assigned to any backup plan. - Required tags are missing or incorrect.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure to create AWS IAM Policies
description: AWS IAM policies, specify the desired permissions for accessing AWS resources and define the conditions under which those permissions are granted. Configure the appropriate policies to keep your resources secure.
rationale: Managing AWS IAM policies is crucial to safeguard your backups from unauthorized access, ensuring that only approved users can manipulate or view sensitive data.
audit: To create a role for AWS Backup, follow these steps: 1. Navigate to the "IAM Dashboard" in the AWS Console. 2. Select "Roles" from the left-hand menu. 3. Click on the "Create Role" button. 4. Choose "AWS Service" as the trusted entity. 5. Select "AWS Backup" as the service that will use this role. 6. Choose a policy to apply to the role or create a custom policy. 7. Review the role details and provide a meaningful name for the role. 8. Click on "Create Role" to finalize the creation of the role for AWS Backup.
remediation: AWS IAM policies, restricting access to backup resources, and implementing additional security measures to prevent future incidents. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies.html
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that AWS IAM policies and roles can be fully queried, inspected, and validated using AWS APIs, boto3 SDK, and AWS CLI. The audit steps (creating and verifying IAM roles and policies for AWS Backup) are entirely accessible via programmatic means, including checking for existence, attachment, and permissions of policies. Additional technical improvements suggested (such as checking policy attachments and inspecting policy documents for overly permissive access) are feasible with available APIs.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: IAM Roles and Policies (specifically for AWS Backup) - API/CLI Method: - `boto3.client('iam').list_roles()` - `boto3.client('iam').get_role(RoleName=...)` - `boto3.client('iam').list_attached_role_policies(RoleName=...)` - `boto3.client('iam').get_policy(PolicyArn=...)` - `boto3.client('iam').get_policy_version(PolicyArn=..., VersionId=...)` - Properties to Check: - Existence of at least one IAM role with AWS Backup as the trusted service principal. - Attached policies to the role that grant only the necessary permissions for AWS Backup operations (least privilege). - Policy documents do not contain overly broad permissions (e.g., `"Effect": "Allow", "Action": "*", "Resource": "*"`) and are scoped to required resources. - Pass Criteria: - There is an IAM role with AWS Backup as the trusted entity. - The role has attached policies granting only required permissions (no wildcards unless strictly necessary). - No attached policy grants excessive permissions. - Fail Criteria: - No role exists for AWS Backup. - Role exists but has no policies attached. - Policies are overly permissive or not scoped to backup resources.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure to create IAM roles for Backup
description: An AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) role is similar to a user, in that it is an AWS identity with permissions policies that determine what the identity can and cannot do in AWS. However, instead of being uniquely associated with one person, a role is intended to be assumable by anyone who needs it.
rationale: While Service Linked Roles offer quick deployment, using default configurations isn't recommended for security best practices.
audit: To create a role for AWS Backup, follow these steps: 1. Navigate to the "IAM Dashboard" in the AWS Console. 2. Select "Roles" from the left-hand menu. 3. Click on the "Create Role" button. 4. Choose "AWS Service" as the trusted entity. 5. Select "AWS Backup" as the service that will use this role. 6. Choose a policy to apply to the role or create a custom policy. 7. Review the role details and provide a meaningful name for the role. 8. Click on "Create Role" to finalize the creation of the role for AWS Backup.
remediation: Assess your organization's needs to determine whether to utilize Service Linked Roles for AWS backups. Default Value: When using the AWS Backup console for the first time, you can choose to have AWS Backup create a default service role for you. This role has the permissions that AWS Backup needs to create and restore backups on your behalf. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/using-service-linked- roles.html
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the existence and configuration of IAM roles for AWS Backup can be fully verified using AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). IAM roles, their trust relationships, and attached policies are all accessible via programmatic means. The audit requires checking for a role assigned to AWS Backup with the appropriate permissions, which is feasible and reliable using automation.
automation_summary: - AWS Resource/Service: IAM Roles (specifically roles trusted by AWS Backup) - API/CLI Method: - boto3: `iam.list_roles()`, `iam.get_role()`, `iam.list_attached_role_policies()`, `iam.get_role_policy()` - AWS CLI: `aws iam list-roles`, `aws iam get-role`, `aws iam list-attached-role-policies`, `aws iam get-role-policy` - Properties to Check: - Existence of at least one IAM role with a trust policy allowing AWS Backup (`backup.amazonaws.com`) as the principal. - Attached policies (managed or inline) granting necessary AWS Backup permissions (e.g., `AWSBackupServiceRolePolicyForBackup` or equivalent custom policy). - Pass Criteria: - At least one IAM role exists with a trust relationship for AWS Backup (`Service: backup.amazonaws.com`). - The role has attached policies that grant the required permissions for AWS Backup operations (either the AWS managed policy or a custom policy with equivalent permissions). - Fail Criteria: - No IAM role exists with a trust relationship for AWS Backup. - The role exists but does not have the necessary permissions attached.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure AWS Backup with Service Linked Roles
description: AWS Service Linked Roles are IAM roles designed specifically for AWS Backup. These roles come with default configurations allowing access to all AWS resources by default.
rationale: While Service Linked Roles offer quick deployment, using default configurations isn't recommended for security best practices.
audit: Create service-linked role for AWS Backup: You don't need to create a service-linked role manually. AWS Backup automatically creates it when you list resources to back up, set up cross-account backup, or perform backups using the AWS Management Console, AWS CLI, or AWS API. If you delete this role, you can recreate it by following the same steps. AWS Backup will create it for you again when needed.
remediation: Assess your organization's needs to determine whether to utilize Service Linked Roles for AWS backups. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/using-service-linked- roles.html 2 Elastic Block Store (EBS) Amazon EBS is a block level file storage system that runs on EC2. EBS can be used as a hard drive that’s mounted on an EC2 instance (virtual machine). EBS can store data as a standalone apart from EC2. This means that data can persist to the block storage service while an EC2 instance is offline. Out of the box EBS functions as an unformatted file system that needs to be configured and mounted on top of an EC2 instance. EBS can be used as both a storage and boot drive; for the purposes of this document, we will focus on EBS as a storage device. EBS comes with many different options to fit the specific needs of an application. EBS is most likely the right choice if you need quick access to read and write files to the cloud and you need these files to be stored long term. You can also rapidly unmount the file system from one EC2 instance and deploy it to another instance by using snapshots.

EBS can be used as a hard drive that’s mounted on an EC2 instance (virtual machine). EBS can store data as a standalone apart from EC2.
